did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid intelligent framework for skin lesion severity identification. The AI methods include a Multi-Scale Vision Transformer (MS-ViT) for deep feature extraction, handcrafted features based on dermatological ABCD rules, and a Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) for classification. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC dataset to classify lesions into mild, moderate, and severe categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid intelligent framework for skin lesion severity identification using a Multi-Scale Vision Transformer (MS-ViT), handcrafted features, and a Fuzzy Inference System. It includes a proposed methodology, experimental results on real-world datasets (ISIC), quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score), and comparisons with baseline models, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Severity Identification of Skin Lesions using Multi-Scale Vision Transformer and Handcrafted Features
journal: Proceedings of the 6th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA-2026)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid intelligent framework for the severity identification of skin lesions, addressing challenges like intra-class variability and diagnostic uncertainty. The method integrates a Multi-Scale Vision Transformer (MS-ViT) for deep feature extraction with handcrafted clinical features based on dermatological ABCD rules and texture descriptors. These combined features are processed by a Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) to handle ambiguity and classify lesions into mild, moderate, and severe categories. The model was evaluated on the ISIC dataset containing 25,321 images. The proposed approach achieved an overall accuracy of 94.53%, with precision, recall, and F1-scores of 93.20%, 91.39%, and 92.79%, respectively. Results indicate that the hybrid framework significantly outperforms traditional CNNs, standalone transformers, and feature-only baselines.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the severity identification of skin lesions in general, classifying them into mild, moderate, and severe categories. The dataset used is the ISIC dataset containing images of various skin diseases.

Mentioned in the introduction and abstract as an example of an aggressive and lethal form of skin cancer to provide context for the importance of early diagnosis, but not explicitly listed as a specific target class for the severity classification task in the experiments.

Mentioned in the abstract as a category of lesions (benign, premalignant, malignant) to provide context, but the experimental task is severity-based (mild, moderate, severe) rather than benign vs. malignant classification.
Mentioned in the abstract as a category of lesions to provide context, but not a specific target class in the experimental severity classification.

Mentioned in the abstract as a category of lesions to provide context, but the study's specific output classes are severity levels (mild, moderate, severe) rather than a binary malignant classification.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion severity levels (mild, moderate, severe)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions into three discrete severity categories: mild, moderate, and severe. The model uses a Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) to classify these categories based on fused deep features from a Multi-Scale Vision Transformer (MS-ViT) and handcrafted clinical features. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A real-world dermoscopic dataset containing images of various skin diseases, used for evaluating the proposed hybrid intelligent framework for skin lesion severity identification.

use.training: Used for training the proposed hybrid framework (MS-ViT + Handcrafted Features + FIS).
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the proposed method against baselines (Inception Net V3, ResNet-50, etc.).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and qualitative statements of superiority (e.g., 'significantly outperforms', 'marked performance improvements') without statistical significance testing.

ISIC dataset.notes: The proposed hybrid framework achieved an overall accuracy of 94.53%, with precision of 93.20%, recall of 91.39%, and F1-score of 92.79%. It outperformed traditional CNNs, standalone transformers, and feature-only baselines. The paper notes a performance increase of more than 7.6% in accuracy when comparing the proposed method to results for handcrafted features alone. Confusion matrix and heatmap analyses indicated strong class separability and robustness.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid intelligent framework combining Multi-Scale Vision Transformer (MS-ViT), handcrafted features, and Fuzzy Inference System (FIS)

architecture_details.backbone_details: Hierarchical feature extractor using patch embedding to represent image areas into tokens. Transformer encoder encodes local and global contextual representations at various scales. Contains Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA) layers, feed-forward networks, residual links, and layer normalization. Combines information from various scales and attention heads.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Handcrafted features based on dermatological ABCD rules and texture descriptors (shape, color, texture).
architecture_details.classification_head: Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) performing fuzzification, rule-based reasoning, and defuzzification to handle ambiguity and uncertainty.

preprocessing_techniques: Removal of artifacts such as hairs from dermoscopic images
conclusions: The proposed hybrid intelligent framework, which integrates a Multi-Scale Vision Transformer (MS-ViT), handcrafted clinical features based on ABCD rules, and a Fuzzy Inference System (FIS), significantly outperforms traditional CNNs, standalone Vision Transformers, and feature-only baselines in skin lesion severity identification.
The model achieved an overall accuracy of 94.53%, precision of 93.20%, recall of 91.39%, and F1-score of 92.79% on the ISIC dataset for classifying lesions into mild, moderate, and severe categories.

Feature fusion of deep learning features from MS-ViT and handcrafted clinical features yields marked performance improvements, providing an accuracy increase of more than 7.6% compared to using handcrafted features alone.

The integration of the Fuzzy Inference System effectively handles ambiguity and uncertainty in medical imagery, enhancing the reliability of severity classification decisions through rule-based reasoning with soft decision boundaries.

MS-ViT demonstrates superior capability in capturing hierarchical and global contextual dependencies compared to traditional CNNs, which struggle with long-range dependencies and intricate lesion patterns.

Purely handcrafted feature-based models exhibit limited discriminative ability due to their reliance on preset rules and texture statistics, whereas the hybrid approach leverages the complementary nature of deep and clinical features.
Confusion matrix and heatmap analyses indicate strong class separability, robustness, and balanced performance across the mild, moderate, and severe severity levels.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (ISIC) with 25,321 images; no external validation or multi-center datasets were used, limiting generalizability claims.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for performance comparisons, relying instead on descriptive metrics.
Key training hyperparameters, optimizer details, loss functions, and input image sizes are not reported in the provided text.
Data augmentation techniques are not explicitly detailed for the proposed model, though related work mentions augmentation for other methods.
The 'Severe' class results for Precision, Recall, and F1-Score are reported as null in the per-class analysis, indicating incomplete reporting for that specific category.
Transformer-based models are noted to be computationally costly and data-hungry, posing potential deployment concerns for resource-constrained clinical settings.
The authors acknowledge that future work is needed to validate the framework on larger multi-center datasets and incorporate lesion segmentation.
The model addresses uncertainty via a Fuzzy Inference System, but the interpretability of the fuzzy rules themselves is not quantitatively evaluated.
